definition: A phylactery is a protective amulet or scripture-case — in Greek and Jewish usage a worn safeguard; in modern fantasy, the vessel hiding a lich's soul.
history: Greek *phylaktērion*, 'a thing that guards,' from *phylassein*, 'to keep watch' — first an amulet, then the Jewish *tefillin*, a scripture-case worn on the body. Modern fantasy reassigned it to a lich's soul-jar, inverting the moral charge while keeping the core image of a small container of sealed power. See the full Word History.

etymology.origin: Greek phylaktērion ‘a safeguard, amulet’, from phylassein ‘to guard’
etymology.note: First the Jewish tefillin, then any protective amulet or scripture-case. Modern fantasy (D&D) reassigned it to a lich’s soul-jar — a total shift of referent while keeping the ‘container of power’ core.
etymology.hook: The amulet that fantasy turned into a soul-jar.

essay.title: Phylactery: the amulet that fantasy turned into a soul-jar
essay.dek: Greek watch-charm, to Jewish prayer-box, to lich's soul-jar — one word, three owners.
essay.html:
Few words have travelled as far, or changed masters as completely, as phylactery. It begins in Greek as phylaktērion — literally 'a thing that guards' — from phylassein, 'to keep watch.' A phylactery was an amulet: a small protective object worn against harm, exactly the kind of charged charm that fills this study's classical-items family.

From there it acquired a religious home. In the Greek of the New Testament, phylaktēria names the tefillin — the small leather boxes of scripture bound to arm and forehead in prayer. Same logic as the amulet: sacred text, sealed in a container, worn on the body. For most of its English life, from the 14th century on, that is what a phylactery was — a scripture-case, or any protective charm.

Then a game rewrote it. In Dungeons & Dragons, a lich hides its soul inside a phylactery; destroy the object and you destroy the deathless wizard. The container-of-holy-power became a container-of-stolen-life — a total inversion of moral charge that keeps the core image perfectly intact: a small object in which something precious is sealed for safekeeping. Magic: The Gathering, notably, tends to keep closer to the older sense; the word remembers what it used to mean, even when we don't.

sources.label: Etymonline / OED, s.v. phylactery (‹ Greek phylaktērion)

flavor_snippets.html:
"In my early experiments in <mark>phylactery</mark>, I found that fragile forms have their uses."
—Rocati, Duke of Martyne

mechanics_snippets.html:
Indestructible
As this creature enters, put a <mark>phylactery</mark> counter on an artifact you control.
When you control no permanents with <mark>phylactery</mark> counters on them, sacrifice this creature.
